question: What is the maximum imprisonment term for an offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act?
ground_truth: The person shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may be extended to two years, or with fine which may extend to twice the amount of the cheque, or with both.
context: ...such person shall be deemed to have committed an offence and shall, without prejudice to any other provisions of this Act, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may be extended to two years, or with fine which may extend to twice the amount of the cheque, or with both.

question: Within what period must a cheque be presented to the bank for Section 138 to apply?
ground_truth: The cheque must be presented to the bank within a period of six months from the date on which it is drawn or within the period of its validity, whichever is earlier.
context: the cheque has been presented to the bank within a period of six months from the date on which it is drawn or within the period of its validity, whichever is earlier

question: If a payee receives information from the bank about a returned cheque but does not send a written demand notice to the drawer, can the drawer be prosecuted under Section 138?
ground_truth: No. Section 138 explicitly requires that the payee makes a demand for payment by giving a notice in writing to the drawer within thirty days of receiving information from the bank about the returned cheque. Without this notice, the proviso conditions are not met and the section does not apply.
context: the payee or the holder in due course of the cheque, as the case may be, makes a demand for the payment of the said amount of money by giving a notice in writing, to the drawer of the cheque, within thirty days of the receipt of information by him from the bank regarding the return of the cheque as unpaid

question: Does Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act apply when a cheque is dishonoured due to stop payment instructions issued by the drawer?
ground_truth: Yes. Section 138 can be attracted when a cheque is dishonoured on account of stop payment instructions sent by the accused to his bank in respect of a post-dated cheque, irrespective of insufficiency of funds in the account.
context: Section 138 of the Act can indeed be attracted when a cheque is dishonoured on account of 'stop payment' instructions sent by the accused to his bank in respect of a post-dated cheque, irrespective of insufficiency of funds in the account.

question: Why should a drawer of a post-dated cheque not be permitted to countermand its payment to avoid liability under Section 138?
ground_truth: Because allowing countermand would render Section 138 a dead letter and permit persons to take advantage of their own wrong. A post-dated cheque is an accommodation given to the drawer by the creditor, and the drawer should not be allowed to abuse that accommodation. Section 139 also raises a presumption that the cheque was issued in discharge of a debt, which the drawer must rebut with evidence.
context: A post-dated cheque will lose its credibility and acceptability if its payment can be stopped routinely... by countermanding payment of a post-dated cheque, a party should not be allowed to get away from the penal provision of Section 138. A contrary view would render S. 138 a dead letter and will provide a handle to persons trying to avoid payment under legal obligations undertaken by them through their own acts which in other words can be said to be taking advantage of one's own wrong.

question: Under Section 139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, a presumption arises in favour of the cheque holder. What standard of proof must the accused meet to rebut this presumption, and must the accused personally testify to do so?
ground_truth: Under Section 139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, a presumption arises in favour of the cheque holder. What standard of proof must the accused meet to rebut this presumption, and must the accused personally testify to do so?
context: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability. + An accused for discharging the burden of proof placed upon him under a statute need not examine himself. He may discharge his burden on the basis of the materials already brought on record... whereas prosecution must prove the guilt of an accused beyond all reasonable doubt, the standard of proof so as to prove a defence on the part of the accused is 'preponderance of probabilities'.

question: According to Krishna Janardhan Bhat v. Dattatraya G. Hegde as cited in Rangappa vs Sri Mohan, does Section 139 raise a presumption about the existence of a legally enforceable debt?
ground_truth: No. Section 139 only raises a presumption that the cheque was issued for the discharge of any debt or other liability. The existence of a legally recoverable debt itself is not a matter of presumption under Section 139.
context: Section 139 of the Act merely raises a presumption in regard to the second aspect of the matter. Existence of legally recoverable debt is not a matter of presumption under Section 139 of the Act. It merely raises a presumption in favour of a holder of the cheque that the same has been issued for discharge of any debt or other liability.

question: What does Section 139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act presume about a cheque holder?
ground_truth: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability.
context: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability.

question: Under Section 141 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, who is deemed guilty when a company commits an offence under Section 138?
ground_truth: Every person who, at the time the offence was committed, was in charge of, and was responsible to the company for the conduct of the business of the company, as well as the company itself, shall be deemed to be guilty of the offence and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly.
context: If the person committing an offence under section 138 is a company, every person who, at the time the offence was committed, was in charge of, and was responsible to the company for the conduct of the business of the company, as well as the company, shall be deemed to be guilty of the offence and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly.

question: Under Section 141, what must a person prove to escape liability for a company's offence under Section 138?
ground_truth: The person must prove either that the offence was committed without his knowledge, or that he had exercised all due diligence to prevent the commission of such offence.
context: nothing contained in this sub-section shall render any person liable to punishment if he proves that the offence was committed without his knowledge, or that he had exercised all due diligence to prevent the commission of such offence.

question: A government-nominated director of a company is in charge of its business at the time a cheque bounces. Can they be prosecuted under Section 141?
ground_truth: No. Section 141 explicitly provides that a person nominated as a Director of a company by virtue of holding any office or employment in the Central Government, State Government, or a financial corporation owned or controlled by either government shall not be liable for prosecution under that chapter.
context: where a person is nominated as a Director of a company by virtue of his holding any office or employment in the Central Government or State Government or a financial corporation owned or controlled by the Central Government or the State Government, as the case may be, he shall not be liable for prosecution under this Chapter.

question: Under Section 142(2) of the Negotiable Instruments Act, which court has territorial jurisdiction to try an offence under Section 138 when the cheque is delivered for collection through an account?
ground_truth: The court within whose local jurisdiction the branch of the bank where the payee or holder in due course maintains the account is situated shall have jurisdiction.
context: The offence under section 138 shall be inquired into and tried only by a court within whose local jurisdiction — if the cheque is delivered for collection through an account, the branch of the bank where the payee or holder in due course, as the case may be, maintains the account, is situated.

question: In Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod, the complainant argued that a contractual clause restricting jurisdiction to Aurangabad courts and the fact that the statutory notice was sent from Aurangabad should give JMFC Aurangabad jurisdiction. Did the court accept this?
ground_truth: No. The court held that principles of cause of action in civil law are not relevant in criminal prosecution. The clause restricting jurisdiction to Aurangabad courts has no bearing on the situs in criminal prosecutions, and the High Court had erred in analyzing the cause of action and the jurisdiction covenant as these are relevant only for civil disputes.
context: The principles pertaining to the cause of action as perceived in civil law are not relevant in criminal prosecution. Whilst the clause restricting jurisdiction to courts at Aurangabad may have efficacy for civil proceedings, provided any part of the cause of action had arisen in Aurangabad, it has no bearing on the situs in criminal prosecutions... the High Court committed an error in analyzing the cause of action as well as the covenant restricting jurisdiction to Aurangabad as these are relevant only for civil disputes.

question: In Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod, the cheque was drawn on a Delhi bank but presented at Aurangabad. Under Section 142(2) of the Negotiable Instruments Act, which court would have jurisdiction to try the offence?
ground_truth: Under Section 142(2), if the cheque is delivered for collection through an account, jurisdiction lies with the court where the branch of the bank in which the payee maintains their account is situated. In Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod, the cheque was presented at Aurangabad but the accused transacted business from Delhi and the cheque was drawn on a Delhi bank. The court upheld the High Court's conclusion that JMFC Aurangabad had no jurisdiction over the offence.
context: The offence under section 138 shall be inquired into and tried only by a court within whose local jurisdiction — if the cheque is delivered for collection through an account, the branch of the bank where the payee or holder in due course, as the case may be, maintains the account, is situated. + The High Court... concludes that the JMFC, Aurangabad has no jurisdiction over the offence described in the Complaint. The Appeal is accordingly dismissed.

question: In Icon Buildcon Pvt. Ltd. vs Aggarwal Developers Pvt. Ltd., on what ground did the trial court acquit the respondents of the offence under Section 138?
ground_truth: The trial court held that at the time the cheques were issued, no legally enforceable liability had arisen because the conditions under the agreements to sell had not been fulfilled, the NOC had not been obtained by the villagers, and the legal notice did not appear to have been properly served on the accused persons.
context: at the time when the cheques in question were issued no legally enforceable liability arose. It was concluded by the learned trial Court that the cheques were not issued for discharging of legal enforceable liability. Finally, the trial Court also held that the legal notice did not appear to be served upon the address of the accused persons and, therefore, there was no proper service of the notice upon them under Section 138 NI Act. Consequently, the Respondents were acquitted of the offence under Section 138 NI Act.

question: In Icon Buildcon, the court held that ADPL's stop payment instructions were bona fide. What facts supported this conclusion?
ground_truth: The court found that IBPL was aware even on the date of the agreement that the properties were subject to litigation. A restraint order had been passed by the SDM on 16 January 2007 restraining the villagers from creating third party interest, and a further restraint order was passed by the High Court on 5 March 2007. Since IBPL's rights to the land were inchoate and contingent on obligations that were never fulfilled, it was not possible for IBPL to convey valid transferable rights to ADPL, making the stop payment instructions bona fide.
context: the depositions of CW-1 and CW-4 reveal that IBPL was aware even on the date of the agreement dated 24th February 2007 between it and ADPL that the properties in question were the subject matter of litigation... on the date when the cheques in question were issued, it was not possible for IBPL to convey to ADPL a valid transferable right in the properties in question... if on coming to know of the above difficulties, the Respondents had issued stop payment instructions, it cannot be said to lack in bona fides.

question: Section 139 raises a presumption in favour of the cheque holder. In Icon Buildcon, the cheques were dishonoured due to stop payment instructions — did this presumption protect the complainant, and what did the drawer need to show to rebut it?
ground_truth: Section 139 raises a presumption in favour of the cheque holder. In Icon Buildcon, the cheques were dishonoured due to stop payment instructions — did this presumption protect the complainant, and what did the drawer need to show to rebut it?
context: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability. + even in a case of dishonour of cheque on account of stop payment instructions, the presumption under Section 139 of NI Act is attracted. However, it is rebuttable. The drawer of the cheque can seek to rebut the presumptions by showing on a preponderance of probabilities i.e., that the stop payment instructions issued were bona fide.

question: In Avneet Luthra vs Sunita Vijay, why was Accused No. 2 acquitted despite being a joint account holder on the dishonoured cheque?
ground_truth: Accused No. 2 was acquitted because he had not signed the dishonoured cheque. The court held that liability under Section 138 of the NI Act can be fastened only on the person who has actually signed the cheque, and mere status as a joint account holder does not attract penal consequences under Section 138 in the absence of a signature on the dishonoured instrument.
context: in the case of a dishonoured cheque drawn from a joint account, liability under Section 138 of the NI Act, can be fastened only on the person who has actually signed the cheque. Mere status as a joint account holder does not attract penal consequences under Section 138 in the absence of a signature on the dishonoured instrument.

question: In Avneet Luthra vs Sunita Vijay, the accused claimed the cheque was issued merely as a security cheque. How did the court address this defence?
ground_truth: The court rejected this defence. It found that the MOU dated 02.12.2021 expressly recorded that the cheque was issued towards repayment of Rs. 29 lakhs advanced by the complainant, and that the accused had signed the MOU after reading and understanding its contents. The court noted it was implausible that a cheque for exactly Rs. 29 lakhs was issued merely as security for future penalties, and that if that were the case, a blank cheque would have sufficed.
context: Ex. CW1/C expressly notes that the cheque in question was issued towards repayment of Rs. 29 lakhs advanced by the complainant... the accused persons have acknowledged that they executed Ex. CW1/C after reading and understanding its contents... It is also implausible that the cheque for exactly Rs. 29 lakhs was issued merely as security for future penalties. If that were the case, a blank cheque would have sufficed.

question: Section 139 raises a rebuttable presumption in favour of the cheque holder. In Avneet Luthra vs Sunita Vijay, once this presumption was activated, what did the accused need to do to discharge the onus, and did she succeed?
ground_truth: Once the presumption under Section 139 is activated, the onus shifts to the accused to rebut it on a standard of preponderance of probabilities. The accused can do so either by leading defence evidence or by specifying lacunae in the complainant's case. In Avneet Luthra, the accused failed to discharge this onus — she did not produce supporting documents such as bank statements, could not substantiate her claim that the cheque was a security cheque, and her admission of partial liability without quantifying it further undermined her defence. The court concluded she failed to create reasonable doubt in the complainant's case.
context: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability. + once the complainant proves that the cheque was drawn/issued by the accused, the mandatory rebuttable presumption under Section 139 of the NI Act gets activated. As soon as the presumption is raised, the onus shifts upon the accused to rebut the presumption on the standard of preponderance of probabilities... the accused has failed in establishing a probable defence on a standard of preponderance of probabilities to rebut the presumption under section 118 and 139 of the NI Act.
